claim: Autonomy spectrum for agents ranges from one-tool agents (minimal autonomy) to looped agents with memory and subtask execution (high autonomy); the right level depends on task accuracy requirements.
actionable: For agent design, place on autonomy spectrum: more autonomy = more fallible but faster; reduce autonomy if accuracy is critical, then iterate.
source_title: Principles of Building AI Agents | Sam Bhagwat, CEO Mastra

claim: Complex prompts should be decomposed into multiple simpler prompts in a workflow graph; instead of diagnosing 12 symptoms in one LLM call, break into 12 separate calls.
actionable: When prompt gets complex (>1000 tokens, 5+ decision branches), decompose into a workflow graph of simpler, focused prompts; increases accuracy and debuggability.
source_title: Principles of Building AI Agents | Sam Bhagwat, CEO Mastra

claim: When building agents, start with OpenAI (default, good performance) unless using code (use Claude) or in long-term need of open-source; avoid over-complicating model selection early.
actionable: For MVP agents, use OpenAI GPT-4; switch to Claude if generating code; only migrate to open-source when infrastructure is proven necessary.
source_title: Principles of Building AI Agents | Sam Bhagwat, CEO Mastra

claim: Tool naming and semantics matter significantly; agents perform better when function names are descriptive (multiply_numbers vs do_stuff) and detailed descriptions + examples are provided.
actionable: Design tool definitions with semantic names, detailed descriptions, and usage examples; poor naming causes agents to misapply tools.
source_title: Principles of Building AI Agents | Sam Bhagwat, CEO Mastra

claim: Multi-agent architectures can use supervisor models, structured workflow graphs with defined handoffs, or agent-chosen workflows; composition patterns matter more than individual agent quality.
actionable: For multi-agent systems, design agent composition explicitly: supervisor pattern for lightweight coordination, workflows for deterministic routing, agent-choice for flexibility.
source_title: Principles of Building AI Agents | Sam Bhagwat, CEO Mastra

claim: RAG is semantic search plus LLM synthesis; it works by embedding and vector-searching chunks, reranking top results, then using LLM to synthesize answers from retrieved context.
actionable: For RAG systems, implement chunking -> embedding -> vector search -> reranking -> synthesis pipeline; reranking is often overlooked but improves final quality.
source_title: Principles of Building AI Agents | Sam Bhagwat, CEO Mastra
